ECHOES: Equal-weight Completed Hypothetical Observation Ensembles

RA axes are wrapped and drawn increasing leftwards so the CMASS-South cap is contiguous and centred near 0. First render fetches the embed engine, so allow a moment.
Observed galaxies: 109,636
Missing fraction: 7.4%
Reliable photometry: 99.3%
Photo-z σNMAD: 0.019
Photo-z PIT mean: 0.503 (0.5 ideal)
Recovered collided: 5,272 / 5,538 implied
Recovered z-failures: 1,505 / 1,982 implied
Angular w(θ) closure: ≈ +0%
z-prior systematic: ≪ statistical
Truth recovery wp(rp): 0.98–1.01
Equal-weight = official: ≈1%
Correction uncertainty: 0.2–0.4% (≪ cosmic var)

Scope: what this product is, and is not

What it is. An ensemble of equal-weight, cosmology-free catalogs of the BOSS DR12 CMASS-South galaxies as they would have been observed had spectroscopic incompleteness (fiber collisions, redshift failures, imaging systematics) been negligible. Every observed galaxy is kept at its spectroscopic (RA, Dec, z); each missing galaxy is added at its real SDSS imaging position with a redshift drawn from a local-density (KNN) posterior. The realizations differ only in the missing galaxies' redshifts, so the ensemble spread is the calibrated uncertainty of the completion. Any summary statistic — w(θ), ξ(Δθ,Δz), wp(rp), multipoles, counts-in-cells, kNN-CDF, higher-order — can be computed directly from the points.

What it is not. It is not a substitute for the survey's sample/cosmic variance: the ensemble quantifies only the additional uncertainty introduced by correcting the catalog (≈0.2–0.4% on wp(rp), well below the 1–7% cosmic variance), so a downstream analysis must still obtain the cosmic covariance the usual way (mocks, jackknife, analytic window). It is not a new cosmological measurement, and it assumes no fiducial cosmology — a fiducial cosmology enters only when we convert z to distance to validate 3-D statistics. The redshift-failure recovery is partial (~75% of the weight-implied count), limited by the CMASS-quality imaging pool.

The problem

A spectroscopic galaxy survey never observes every targeted galaxy. In BOSS CMASS three effects remove galaxies in a clustering-dependent way:

  • Fiber collisions — two galaxies closer than the 62″ fiber-placement limit cannot both be observed on a single plate; one is dropped. This preferentially removes close pairs, biasing small-scale clustering.
  • Redshift failures — a spectrum is taken but no reliable redshift is measured.
  • Imaging systematics — stellar density, seeing and extinction modulate the detection efficiency across the sky.

The standard correction up-weights surviving galaxies (WEIGHT_CP, WEIGHT_NOZ, WEIGHT_SYSTOT). Weights make the mean two-point statistics unbiased, but they are not a catalog: they cannot be fed to estimators that assume equal-weight points, they implicitly assume every missing galaxy sits at its nearest neighbour's redshift, and they carry no error model for that assumption. We instead build equal-weight completed catalogs.

The opportunity

BOSS targets were selected from SDSS DR8 ugriz imaging. Every spectroscopically-missing galaxy therefore has a real photometric detection — a known angular position and colours. The incompleteness is almost entirely in the redshift dimension. So rather than guess where missing galaxies are, we place each at its measured position and assign a redshift drawn from a photometric-redshift posterior built from its colours, refined by the observed close-pair statistics. Everything stays in observed coordinates (RA, Dec, z): no fiducial cosmology, no comoving distances. The full 2-D clustering ξ(Δθ,Δz) — which carries the Alcock–Paczynski geometric signal — is preserved as measured, not assumed.

The method

For each realization of the completed catalog:

  1. Keep every observed galaxy at its spectroscopic (RA, Dec, z).
  2. Add each missing galaxy at its real imaging position. The number and identity of missing galaxies are tied to the survey weight bookkeeping (a survivor with WEIGHT_CP=k claims its k−1 nearest unmatched photometric neighbours within 62″; redshift failures are tied to WEIGHT_NOZ analogously).
  3. Assign a redshift by sampling the per-object photo-z posterior p(z|colours); for collided pairs this is multiplied by the empirical close-pair Δz distribution (physical pairs sit near the host redshift, projections do not).
  4. Imaging systematics (WEIGHT_SYSTOT) are applied as a per-object Poisson multiplicity on the whole set.

Because the observed galaxies are fixed and only the missing ~7% vary — and only in their redshifts — the spread across realizations is the genuine, calibrated posterior uncertainty of the correction, which is exactly what a downstream analysis marginalises over. The photo-z is a dependency-light k-nearest-neighbour estimator in colour space returning the empirical neighbour-redshift distribution; it is trained on the good-redshift galaxies the survey already provides.

Approaches we tried first and discarded: generating the field from scratch with a log-Gaussian Cox process / measured 2-D kernel reproduced the mean clustering but its realization covariance was far too large (the high small-scale variance σ²≈4 of the log-normal). Conditioning on the real observed galaxies and completing only the missing fraction removes that problem entirely.

The data

What the corrected catalogs look like

What we measure

Truth recovery: do we get a known answer back?

The closure tests above confirm the completed catalog reproduces the completeness-weighted observed clustering — but that is partly true by construction. The decisive test is inject-and-recover: take a catalog whose true clustering is known, apply a realistic forward model of the BOSS systematics (angular selection from the real randoms, n(z), 62″ fiber collisions, density-coupled redshift failures, WEIGHT_SYSTOT imaging modulation), run the completion on the resulting mock-observed catalog, and check we recover the input truth — not the weighted observation. We do this on a real-BOSS-truth mock (which carries the real 1-halo clustering) and on independent N-body MultiDark-Patchy mocks.

The local-density redshift (the key fix). A raw photo-z posterior has σz≈0.03 (~90 Mpc/h), which smears the line-of-sight pair counts and drives wp(rp) 3–4% low. Instead of drawing from the photo-z alone — or collapsing each missing galaxy onto its nearest neighbour's redshift, which is unphysically sharp — we draw from p(z | n̂, colours) ∝ (1+δg(n̂,z)) · n̄(z) · pphotoz, where (1+δg)·n̄ is a kernel estimate of the local redshift density built from the K nearest observed spectroscopic galaxies along the sightline. This default engine is a fast, cosmology-free KNN approximation to a conditional Gaussian-process field — not a GP itself. The actual graphGP Matheron posterior is available as a separate engine; a head-to-head on real CMASS shows the two recover the same clustering to ~1%, so the KNN proxy is the default (it is also what compresses to the 2 MB shareable package). The photo-z picks the right peak; the local galaxy field sharpens it to the physical clustering scale; no cosmology is assumed. This is what brings wp(rp), the redshift-space multipoles ξ0/ξ2, the higher-order statistics and the angular w(θ) all back to truth at once.

Higher-order and coincidence-sensitive statistics

Two-point closure can hide higher-order errors — in particular the Δθ=0 duplicate spike a naïve WEIGHT_SYSTOT multiplicity (np.repeat) would create. We replaced that with local-analog systot additions (nearby real galaxies with ~1″ jitter, never exact duplicates, and never dropping a real galaxy), and test the completion on statistics that would expose any residual.

Is the ensemble a calibrated posterior?

Each realization fixes every observed galaxy and re-draws only the redshifts of the missing ~9%. The realization-to-realization spread is therefore the uncertainty of the correction; we measure it against the true sample (cosmic) variance from the mock-to-mock scatter.

Consistency with the official weighted analysis

The community measures CMASS clustering with the official completeness weights w_c = WEIGHT_SYSTOT·(WEIGHT_CP+WEIGHT_NOZ−1). Our equal-weight completed catalogs should give the same clustering with no weights — a drop-in, weight-free replacement. A fiducial (Planck18) cosmology is used only to turn redshifts into distances for this test; the catalogs themselves stay cosmology-free.

The strong form — uniform randoms, no weights. An equal-weight catalog should also work with a trivial random: uniform over the footprint, no completeness weighting. Testing this head-on (cross-checked against the BOSS mangle mask) shows CMASS-South is ~99% complete (COMP≈0.99 at the galaxies) — so there is essentially no angular completeness window to remove, and the survey random already is a uniform-footprint random to ~1%. Used that way the completed catalog reproduces the official weighted w(θ) to ~1.5% and wp/ξ₀ to ~1–2%. A separately-constructed uniform random matches only to the precision with which it resolves the survey boundary — a control on the unbiased official data shows the same residual, confirming it is the window construction, not the catalog (and the mask_DR12v5 geometry mask is ~40% larger in area than the LSS clustering footprint, so uniform randoms must be clipped to the LSS region).

Survey mask and inpainting

The completion above corrects galaxies missing from observed area. It does not touch interior mask holes — bright-star masks, bad fields and tiling gaps where there is no data at all (no galaxies, no randoms, no usable imaging). For clustering measured against masked randoms these holes cancel and need no action. But a theorist who wants a hole-free, equal-weight catalog can have one: we fill each hole by transplanting real galaxies — with their colours, magnitudes and local spatial configuration — from environment-matched nearby clean regions, setting each hole's count so its galaxy/random ratio matches its surrounding collar. This is data resampling, not a field model: higher-order clustering and the colour/luminosity structure transfer by construction, and it stays cosmology-free.

To show why the holes exist and how well the transplant works, here are 12 interior holes — 8 bright-star masks (identified by cross-matching hole centres against Gaia) and the rest bad-field/tiling gaps — one per row, with three views each: left, the actual SDSS ugriz imaging the photometric catalogue and BOSS spectroscopic targets were drawn from — a heavily saturated bright star (with bleed trails and halos) or a bad field is plainly the reason the region was masked; middle, the observed galaxies around the empty region (the gap is visible); right, after inpainting, with the transplanted galaxies in blue at 70% opacity so any overlap with real galaxies shows. The fill follows the surrounding density and carries real redshifts and colours.

Selection coupling and validation

A forward-model field reconstruction in the same collaboration (Wechsler, v0 pipeline) raises a question our correction must answer: is the spectroscopic selection itself density-coupled — does the rate at which a targeted galaxy goes missing depend on the local galaxy overdensity? If it is and the correction ignores it, the missing galaxies imprint spurious large-scale power (the mechanism behind the historical MegaZ excess; Thomas et al. 2011). We measure the coupling directly and cosmology-free: at every member of the total target sample (spectroscopic successes plus a given failure kind) we evaluate the local success overdensity δ from an angular aperture, normalised by the random catalogue (so δ is a true overdensity needing no distances and automatically footprint/completeness-aware), then fit the success indicator against δ with a logistic model. The slope h is the coupling; a label-shuffle null fixes its zero point.

Scatter and systematics

Systematics budget

Per effect: the mechanism, how the completion corrects it, the residual after correction, and how that residual was validated.

EffectMechanismCorrectionResidualValidation
Fiber collisionsclose pairs (<62″) dropped; small-scale deficitadd at real imaging position; redshift from local-density (KNN) field × close-pair Δz prior<1–2% on wp(rp)truth recovery; kNN-CDF; scale 40–90″ <0.6%
Redshift failuresspectrum taken, no reliable zadd at real position; redshift from local-density (KNN) field posteriorpartial (~75% of weight-implied); <1% on wptruth recovery; failure-population photo-z audit; coupling test (uncoupled)
Imaging systematicsstellar density / seeing / extinction modulate detection (WEIGHT_SYSTOT)local-analog multiplicity (no duplicates); all real galaxies keptno Δθ=0 artifact; count mode <0.1% on wpkNN-CDF (no 1-NN spike); CIC
Redshift assignmentmissing galaxies have no spec-zGP / local-density posterior p(z | n̂, colours)0–2% on wp/ξ; dominant residual leveroracle test (0.997–1.005); sensitivity (the only >1% lever)
Interior mask holesbright stars / bad fields / tiling gapsoptional analog transplant, surrounding-density matched (cosmology-free)~1% closure; edge halos not filledmasked vs hole-filled w(θ) closure
Correction uncertaintywhich missing galaxies / which redshiftsensemble of realizations0.2–0.4% on wp (≪ cosmic variance)coverage vs Patchy mock-to-mock variance

What this means

The completion produces equal-weight, cosmology-free, configuration-space catalogs that (i) reproduce the completeness-weighted n(z) and angular clustering, (ii) preserve the 2-D ξ(Δθ,Δz) geometry uniformly in redshift, and (iii) come as ensembles whose spread is the genuine, calibrated uncertainty of the missing-galaxy correction. Because they are real catalogs of points, any summary statistic — w(θ), ξ(Δθ,Δz), counts-in-cells, higher-order — can be computed from them, and the systematic budget of the correction is obtained simply by scanning the realizations. The ~7% small-scale difference from the standard weighting is not an error but an imaging-informed improvement: it removes the weights' built-in assumption that every missing galaxy lies at its neighbour's redshift.

Assumptions and limitations, stated plainly: the photo-z is trained on good-redshift galaxies and applied to the missing ones (mildly optimistic for redshift failures, which correlate with low S/N); the z-failure recovery is partial (~75% of the weight-implied count) because not every failure has CMASS-quality photometry; the integral-constraint/window effects are negligible at θ<2° for this footprint but would matter on larger scales; and the close-pair prior is measured from surviving pairs, assumed representative of collided pairs.

Data release

Draw your own samples (≈2 MB). Because every realization shares the same observed galaxies and only the missing ~9% vary, the entire posterior ships as one small file + a standalone sampler — no need to download a multi-GB ensemble. A realization is just a seed (~500/s); a reproducible K-catalog ensemble is K seeds. This is ~700× smaller than a stored ensemble (2 MB vs ~1.4 GB for 1000 realizations) and reproduces the full completion to n(z)~1%, w(θ)~0.1%. Downloads: cmass_south_posterior.npz (~2 MB) · cmass_south_randoms.npz (~5 MB) · draw_samples.py · README.
from draw_samples import load_package, draw
pkg = load_package("cmass_south_posterior.npz")
cat = draw(pkg, seed=0)   # dict(ra, dec, z, prov) — ~120k equal-weight galaxies

Materialized FITS products are also available (loadable with astropy) in two complementary forms:

  • Ensemble (ensemble/realization_*.fits): N equal-weight realizations — columns RA, DEC, Z, PROV (provenance). Use the full ensemble to propagate the correction uncertainty through any statistic.
  • Summary (summary.fits): one catalog of the always-included galaxies with RA, DEC, Z, Z_ERR (per-object redshift uncertainty), PROV and WEIGHT_SYSTOT.
  • Randoms (randoms.fits) and a plain-text PROVENANCE.txt.

Provenance flags label each object: observed-specz, collided, zfail, systot-analog, zhost-fallback, inpaint. The column dictionary, units, conventions and the FKP / integral-constraint / window guidance for users are in DATA_MODEL.md; the pinned, reproducible environment is in environment.yml. The catalogs are in observed coordinates (RA, Dec, z) and carry no fiducial cosmology.

Future extensions and other datasets

  • Improve redshift-failure recovery by relaxing the colour selection for the z-failure pool, or modelling the S/N-dependent failure probability.
  • Radial / Alcock–Paczynski summaries, where the redshift-prior systematic is non-negligible (unlike w(θ)) — propagate it through the realization ensemble.
  • Independent cross-check with bitwise / PIP weights on surveys that provide fiber-assignment realizations (eBOSS, DESI) — BOSS DR12 does not ship them.
  • Other samples and surveys: BOSS CMASS-North and LOWZ; eBOSS LRG/ELG/QSO; DESI. The method needs only matched imaging (positions + colours) and the survey completeness bookkeeping.

Reproduction

PYTHONPATH=/home/tabel/Projects/graphgp:/home/tabel/Projects/graphGP-cosmology \
OMP_NUM_THREADS=16 ~/.venv/k3d/bin/python3 demos/build_completion_presentation.py

# core code: twopt_density/{boss,photoz,cmass_targets,observed_ls}.py
# target fetch: demos/fetch_cmass_targets.py (SDSS DR12 SkyServer)

The graphGP route: a conditional Gaussian-process redshift field

The default completion (other tab) assigns each missing galaxy's redshift from a fast local-density (KNN) estimate along its sightline. This project is named for a more powerful, more flexible engine — graphGP, a scalable nearest-neighbour (Vecchia) Gaussian process — and it is available as a first-class drop-in. This tab explains it and shows the head-to-head.

Switch engines with one argument:
from twopt_density.observed_ls import complete_catalog_photoz, build_gp_field
field = build_gp_field(cat, n_samples=20)          # conditional GP posterior, built once
cat_s = complete_catalog_photoz(cat, tg, pz, z_mode='graphgp', gp_field=field, seed=s)
The default stays z_mode='field' (KNN); 'graphgp' is the opt-in flexible engine that other surveys will want.

What it does

Instead of a per-object KNN kernel density, graphGP draws a full conditional posterior of the galaxy density field δ(n̂,z) given the observed galaxies, via Matheron's pathwise-conditioning rule on a Vecchia graph (twopt_density/density_field.py). Each missing galaxy's redshift is then drawn from p(z | n̂, colours) ∝ (1+δ(n̂,z)) · n̄(z) · pphotoz with the GP field evaluated along its sightline — so neighbouring missing galaxies are correlated through the shared field draw (the KNN proxy treats them independently). The kernel ξ(r) is measured from the data (no ΛCDM / BAO / growth assumed), and the output is in observed redshift.

It is fully data-driven — no cosmology prior

The field engine converts z→comoving distance with a fiducial cosmology to build the neighbour graph and measure ξ(r). We tested whether that injects a cosmological prior by repeating the whole assignment under two radically different fiducials — Planck (Ωm=0.315) and Einstein–de Sitter (Ωm=1.0).

Head-to-head with the KNN default (real CMASS-South)

But which is more faithful to truth? (inject-and-recover)

When to use which

z_mode='field' (default, KNN)z_mode='graphgp'
methodlocal-density KNN-KDEconditional anisotropic GP (Matheron)
best atsub-Mpc fiber-collision scalelarge scales; correlated field-level posterior
cosmologynonefiducial is a validated gauge (<0.1%) → still data-driven
cost~seconds; 2 MB inverse-CDF package~minutes/ensemble; correlated draws (build + sample)
truth recovery~1–2% (sharp small-scale)~1–2% (smooth large-scale)

The KNN engine is the default and what backs the 2 MB shareable package (its per-object independence is what compresses). graphGP is the principled, correlated, more flexible alternative — recommended for field-level inference and for other surveys where the KNN proxy is not enough. Reproduce: demos/graphgp_vs_knn_zfield.py, graphgp_truth_recovery.py, graphgp_cosmology_invariance.py.
